title: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract

items.subtopic: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Leading Risk Factor for Bladder Cancer in the U.S.
items.content: Tobacco smoking is the predominant risk factor for bladder cancer within the United States, with 90% of new cases occurring in current or former smokers. This highlights the importance of smoking cessation as a preventive measure against bladder cancer.
keyPoints: 90% of new bladder cancer diagnoses occur in current or former smokers
Tobacco smoking is the leading risk factor for bladder cancer in the U.S.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Bladder Cancer Epidemiology in the United States
items.content: Bladder cancer is a significant health concern, ranking as the sixth most common cancer diagnosis annually in the U.S., with 82,290 new cases and 16,710 deaths each year. This data underscores the need for awareness and early detection strategies.
keyPoints: Bladder cancer is the sixth most common cancer diagnosis annually in the U.S.
There are 82,290 new cases and 16,710 deaths from bladder cancer every year
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Environmental Risk Factors for Bladder Cancer in Developing Countries
items.content: In developing countries, environmental factors such as arsenic-contaminated water supplies and schistosomiasis infections are major contributors to bladder cancer. These risk factors highlight the need for improved public health measures and infrastructure.
keyPoints: Arsenic contamination and schistosomiasis are significant carcinogenic contributors in developing countries
Environmental exposures play a crucial role in bladder cancer incidence outside of developed nations
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.title: Painless Hematuria as a Red Flag for Urinary Tract Cancer
items.content: Painless hematuria is often the initial manifestation of urinary tract cancer, particularly in males where it is almost always abnormal. In females, it may be mistaken for other conditions like UTIs or menstrual bleeding.
keyPoints: Painless hematuria is a significant indicator of underlying urinary tract cancer.
In males, painless hematuria should prompt further investigation due to its abnormal nature.
Initial investigations include urine cytology and cystoscopy; however, cytology only identifies 50% of high-grade bladder cancers.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.title: Diagnostic Workup for Suspected Urinary Tract Cancer
items.content: For patients with suspected urinary tract cancer, a comprehensive diagnostic workup is essential. This includes urine cytology, cystoscopy, and radiographic evaluation of the kidneys and upper urinary tracts by CT urogram or MR urogram.
keyPoints: Urine cytology identifies cancer in 50% of high-grade bladder cancers.
CT urogram or MR urogram is used for radiographic evaluation; MR urogram is preferred if renal function is poor.
Advanced molecular tests may detect some cancers missed by traditional methods but can also yield false positives.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.title: Understanding Urothelial Carcinoma Histology and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Urothelial carcinoma is the predominant histological type of urinary tract cancer, accounting for approximately 90% of cases. Variants such as micropapillary and plasmacytoid are associated with worse surgical outcomes compared to typical urothelial carcinoma.
keyPoints: Urothelial carcinoma accounts for ~90% of urinary tract cancers.
Variant histologies like micropapillary and plasmacytoid correlate with poorer surgical outcomes.
Nonurothelial variants (e.g., squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma) make up ≤10% of cases.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · HISTOLOGY | MOLECULAR BIOLOGY

items.title: Molecular Characteristics and Clinical Implications in Urothelial Carcinoma
items.content: Urothelial carcinoma of the bladder exhibits a biphasic phenotype with low-grade tumors often recurring but rarely invading, while high-grade tumors invade early. Key molecular alterations include FGFR3 mutations (60–80%) in low-grade tumors and TP53/RB1 mutations in high-grade tumors.
keyPoints: Low-grade urothelial carcinoma frequently recurs but seldom invades; high-grade often invades early.
FGFR3 mutations or gene fusions are present in 60–80% of low-grade tumors.
High-grade tumors show TP53 and RB1 mutations, alterations in CDH1, increased VEGFR2 expression.
10–20% of upper urinary tract urothelial carcinoma may be linked to Lynch syndrome.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · HISTOLOGY | MOLECULAR BIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Bladder Cancer Staging and Survival Rates
items.content: Bladder cancer staging is determined by the depth of tumor invasion, lymph node involvement, and distant metastasis. Non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) accounts for 75% of cases, while muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC) involves deeper layers such as the muscularis propria. Survival rates vary significantly with stage: early stages have high survival rates, whereas advanced stages show poor outcomes.
keyPoints: Approximately 75% of bladder cancer is non-muscle-invasive (NMIBC).
Muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC) involves invasion into the muscularis propria or beyond.
5-year overall survival rates: 70–90% for stage I-II, 39–50% for stage III, and 8% for stage IV.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · STAGING AND OUTCOMES BY STAGE

items.title: Criteria for Bladder Cancer Staging and Implications on Treatment
items.content: The staging of bladder cancer is primarily based on the T stage, with additional considerations for lymph node involvement (N) and distant metastasis (M). NMIBC involves only superficial layers, while MIBC extends into deeper structures. Lymph node involvement classifies as stage III disease, and any distant spread qualifies as stage IV, significantly impacting prognosis.
keyPoints: NMIBC is defined by tumors confined to the epithelial layer or connective tissue below (T1).
MIBC involves invasion into the muscularis propria (T2) or beyond.
Lymph node involvement: N1 (solitary node), N2 (two nodes), N3 (common iliac nodes).
Stage III disease includes regional lymph node involvement; stage IV includes distant metastasis.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · STAGING AND OUTCOMES BY STAGE

items.title: Intravesical Therapy for NMIBC
items.content: For non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC), intravesical therapy is used to reduce recurrence and progression. Mitomycin C, epirubicin, or gemcitabine instilled within 24 hours of TURBT reduces early relapses by 12% in low-risk patients. BCG therapy decreases recurrence from 50% to 29% and progression to muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC) by 27%.
keyPoints: Intravesical mitomycin C, epirubicin, or gemcitabine reduces early relapses by 12% in low-risk NMIBC when instilled within 24 hours of TURBT.
BCG therapy decreases recurrence from 50% to 29% and progression to MIBC by 27% for intermediate- or high-risk tumors.
Side effects of BCG include dysuria, urinary frequency, bladder spasms, hematuria, and rare systemic inflammatory response (<5%).
Maintenance BCG treatments further reduce NMIBC recurrence compared to induction alone.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.title: Management of Recurrent NMIBC Post-BCG
items.content: For NMIBC that recurs after initial BCG treatment, a repeat course can be considered. If recurrence occurs after a second adequate BCG course or within 6 months of initial exposure, cystectomy is recommended due to high progression risk to MIBC and metastatic disease.
keyPoints: Repeat BCG course for NMIBC recurring long after initial treatment.
Cystectomy recommended if recurrence follows a second adequate BCG course or relapse occurs within 6 months of initial BCG.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.title: Bladder-Sparing Therapy for Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer
items.content: In selected patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer without metastasis, bladder-sparing combined-modality therapy can achieve cure in ~65% of cases. This involves concurrent chemotherapy and radiation after maximal debulking by TURBT.
keyPoints: Bladder-sparing therapy achieves a cure rate of ~65% for selected muscle-invasive bladder cancer patients without metastasis.
Concurrent chemotherapy (e.g., cisplatin, carboplatin) and radiation are used post-TURBT debulking.
Regular cystoscopic monitoring is required post-therapy with salvage cystectomy for MIBC development.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.title: Urinary Diversion Options Post-Cystectomy
items.content: After bladder removal, three main urinary diversion options are available: ileostomy, continent urinary reservoir ('Indiana pouch'), and neobladder. The choice depends on patient preference, tumor anatomy, and urologist expertise.
keyPoints: Ileostomy involves connecting ureters to a portion of the ileum that drains urine externally.
Indiana pouch creates an internal reservoir from the ileum for catheterization several times daily.
Neobladder connects the urinary reservoir to the urethra, allowing natural voiding.
All options lead to significant catabolic metabolic changes post-surgery.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.title: Role of Chemotherapy in Bladder Cancer Treatment
items.content: Neoadjuvant and adjuvant chemotherapy with cisplatin-based regimens improve survival outcomes. Cisplatin-containing regimens are superior, offering a 5–10% absolute overall survival advantage.
keyPoints: Cisplatin-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy provides a significant survival benefit pre-surgery.
Non-cisplatin regimens are less effective than cisplatin-containing ones.
Adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy post-surgery further reduces recurrence rates by 55%.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.title: Immunotherapy in High-Risk Urothelial Carcinoma
items.content: Adjuvant PD-1-targeting immunotherapy, such as nivolumab, improves disease-free survival by 30% compared to observation in high-risk bladder cancer patients post-surgery.
keyPoints: Nivolumab is recommended for high-risk postsurgery pathology features.
PD-1 inhibitors provide a significant improvement in disease-free survival.
Immunotherapy is considered standard treatment after surgery for high-risk cases.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.title: Preferred First-Line Therapy for Metastatic Urothelial Carcinoma
items.content: Enfortumab vedotin combined with pembrolizumab (EV+P) is now the preferred first-line therapy for metastatic urothelial carcinoma, replacing platinum-based chemotherapy. This shift was based on a phase 3 clinical trial demonstrating significantly improved median overall survival.
keyPoints: EV+P regimen showed a median overall survival of 31.5 months vs. 16.2 months with platinum-based chemotherapy.
FDA approved EV+P as first-line therapy in 2023 for metastatic urothelial carcinoma.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 3)

items.title: Toxicity Profiles Impacting Treatment Decisions
items.content: The choice of treatment regimen for metastatic urothelial carcinoma is influenced by differing toxicity profiles. Platinum-based chemotherapy commonly causes renal insufficiency, myelosuppression, nausea, and neuropathy. EV+P can cause dose-limiting neuropathy and rare but serious skin toxicity and new-onset diabetes events. Sacituzumab govitecan often leads to myelosuppression and significant diarrhea. PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors have rare severe immune-related toxicities.
keyPoints: Platinum-based chemotherapy: renal insufficiency, myelosuppression, nausea, neuropathy.
EV+P: dose-limiting neuropathy, serious skin toxicity, new-onset diabetes events.
Sacituzumab govitecan: myelosuppression, significant diarrhea requiring IV fluids.
PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors: rare severe immune-related toxicities (e.g., colitis, pneumonitis).
Erdafitinib side effects include hyperphosphatemia, dystrophic nail changes, and central serous retinopathy.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 3)

items.question: What is the median age at diagnosis for bladder cancer?
items.explanation: The median age at diagnosis for bladder cancer is 73 years.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.question: Which demographic group has a higher incidence of bladder cancer?
items.explanation: Bladder cancer is more common in Caucasians than in Asian patients.

items.question: What percentage of new bladder cancer cases occur in current or former smokers?
items.explanation: Among new bladder cancer diagnoses, 90% of cases occur in current or former smokers.

items.question: What represents the initial manifestation of an underlying urinary tract cancer for most patients?
options: Significant cachexia and widespread metastatic disease
items.explanation: For most patients, painless hematuria represents the initial manifestation of an underlying urinary tract cancer.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.question: Which diagnostic test is successful in identifying high-grade bladder cancers only 50% of the time?
items.explanation: Cytology is successful in identifying cancer in only 50% of individuals with high-grade bladder cancers.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.question: Which imaging technique may be substituted for CT urogram in patients with poor renal function?
items.explanation: An MR urogram may be substituted for CT urogram in patients with poor renal function.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.question: Which histological variant of urothelial carcinoma is associated with worse surgical outcomes?
items.explanation: The presence of micropapillary and plasmacytoid variant histologies in urothelial carcinoma is associated with worse surgical outcomes.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · HISTOLOGY | MOLECULAR BIOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of urinary tract tumors are accounted for by nonurothelial variant histologies?
items.explanation: Nonurothelial variant histologies account for ≤10% of urinary tract tumors.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · HISTOLOGY | MOLECULAR BIOLOGY

items.question: Which molecular alteration is characteristic of low-grade urothelial carcinoma?
items.explanation: Low-grade tumors are characterized by FGFR3 mutations or gene fusions.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · HISTOLOGY | MOLECULAR BIOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of bladder cancer cases present with non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC)?
items.explanation: Approximately 75% of bladder cancer presents as NMIBC.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · STAGING AND OUTCOMES BY STAGE

items.question: Which stage of bladder cancer is defined by tumors that invade into the muscularis propria?
items.explanation: Muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC) is defined by tumors that invade into the muscularis propria, which corresponds to stage T2.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · STAGING AND OUTCOMES BY STAGE

items.question: What is the 5-year overall survival rate for patients with stage IV bladder cancer?
items.explanation: The 5-year overall survival rate for stage IV bladder cancer, which extends to metastatic sites, is only 8%.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · STAGING AND OUTCOMES BY STAGE

items.question: Which intravesical therapy is recommended for patients with intermediate- or high-risk NMIBC to reduce the risk of recurrence?
items.explanation: Weekly intravesical instillations of BCG reduce the risk of recurrence from 50% to 29% in intermediate- or high-risk NMIBC.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for patients with NMIBC that recurs long after initial BCG treatment?
items.explanation: A repeat course of BCG can be considered for NMIBC that recurs long after initial treatment.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.question: Which therapy is used to treat low-risk urothelial carcinoma of the renal pelvis or ureter?
items.explanation: Low-risk tumors can be treated by laser ureteroscopic ablation.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.question: What is the approximate cure rate for bladder-sparing combined-modality therapy in muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma of the bladder?
items.explanation: Bladder-sparing combined-modality therapy can achieve cure in approximately 65% of patients.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.question: Which urinary diversion involves connecting the ureters to a portion of ileum that is brought up beneath the abdominal wall muscles, allowing patients to catheterize several times per day?
items.explanation: The Indiana pouch involves connecting the ureters to a portion of ileum that is brought up beneath the abdominal wall muscles, allowing catheterization.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.question: What is the absolute overall survival advantage when using cisplatin-containing neoadjuvant chemotherapy regimens before surgery for MIBC patients?
items.explanation: Meta-analyses have shown a 5–10% absolute overall survival advantage with cisplatin-containing neoadjuvant chemotherapy regimens.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.question: Which treatment option is recommended for high-risk bladder and upper tract urothelial cancer patients post-surgery to improve disease-free survival?
items.explanation: The PD-1-targeting immune checkpoint inhibitor, nivolumab, has demonstrated a 30% improvement in disease-free survival compared to observation.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended approach for patients with poor renal function who are not suitable candidates for cisplatin administration?
options: Proceed directly to surgery and forego neoadjuvant therapy
Delay surgery until renal function improves
Administer BCG therapy via a nephrostomy tube
items.explanation: Patients not suitable for cisplatin administration due to poor renal function should proceed directly to surgery and forego neoadjuvant therapy.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.question: What is the preferred first-line therapy for metastatic urothelial carcinoma as of 2023?
items.explanation: As of 2023, the preferred first-line therapy for metastatic urothelial carcinoma is enfortumab vedotin combined with pembrolizumab (EV+P).
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 3)

items.question: Which treatment option is recommended as a third-line therapy for patients who progress after both platinum-based chemotherapy and a PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint inhibitor?
items.explanation: Sacituzumab govitecan is recommended as a third-line therapy for patients who progress after both platinum-based chemotherapy and a PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint inhibitor.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 3)

items.question: What are common side effects of sacituzumab govitecan?
items.explanation: Common side effects of sacituzumab govitecan include myelosuppression and significant diarrhea requiring intravenous fluid support.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 3)

items.statement: High-grade urothelial carcinoma is characterized by activating FGFR3 mutations.

items.statement: Stage III bladder cancer involves regional lymph nodes in the true pelvis or along the common iliac artery.
items.explanation: Involvement of regional lymph nodes in the true pelvis or along the common iliac artery qualifies as stage III disease.

items.statement: The staging of bladder cancer is primarily driven by the N stage.
items.explanation: The staging of bladder cancer is driven primarily by the T stage, not the N stage.

items.statement: Intravesical BCG treatment is associated with a systemic inflammatory response in more than 5% of cases.
items.explanation: Systemic inflammatory responses occur in rare cases (<5%).

items.statement: Cystectomy is recommended for patients with recurrence after a second adequate course of BCG or relapsed NMIBC within 6 months.

items.statement: All patients undergoing bladder removal experience a significant catabolic change in metabolism.

items.assertion: Smoking is a major risk factor for bladder cancer.

items.assertion: Bladder cancer incidence is higher in underdeveloped countries due to industrial chemical exposure.

items.explanation: The assertion is false as the passage attributes higher bladder cancer incidence in underdeveloped countries to arsenic and schistosomiasis, not industrial chemical exposure.

items.assertion: Small tumors, particularly flat noninvasive tumors of the bladder, may be detected at higher rates with blue light cystoscopy.
items.reason: Blue light and narrow-band imaging cystoscopies are now used routinely in monitoring patients with bladder cancer.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains why small tumors can be detected more effectively.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP

items.assertion: Testing for germline mutations in MLH1, MSH2, or MSH6 is recommended in patients with upper urinary tract urothelial carcinoma under the age of 60.
items.reason: This recommendation applies to those with a first-degree relative diagnosed with Lynch syndrome-associated cancer under the age of 50 or two such relatives regardless of age.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · HISTOLOGY | MOLECULAR BIOLOGY

items.assertion: Clinical outcomes for bladder cancer correlate closely with staging at diagnosis.
items.reason: 5-year overall survival rates vary significantly between stages, from 70–90% for stage I-II to only 8% for stage IV.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why clinical outcomes correlate with staging.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · STAGING AND OUTCOMES BY STAGE

items.assertion: A maximal debulking by TURBT is required before initiating combined-modality therapy for muscle-invasive bladder cancer.
items.reason: This ensures the removal of all visible tumors prior to chemotherapy and radiation.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.assertion: Partial cystectomy can be performed in all patients with muscle-invasive bladder cancer.
items.reason: It allows for resection of tumors while maintaining near physiologic urinary function.
items.explanation: A is false because partial cystectomy is only suitable for a small subset of MIBC patients.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 1)

items.assertion: Cystectomy alone cures half of the patients undergoing the procedure.
items.reason: Despite aggressive surgery, only half of patients are cured by surgery alone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.assertion: Nephroureterectomy is preferred for low-risk urothelial carcinoma of the upper urinary tract.
items.reason: Resection of the kidney and ureter is recommended for high-risk cases to prevent recurrence.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, as nephroureterectomy is preferred for high-risk cases, not low-risk.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 2)

items.assertion: The FDA approved the EV+P regimen in first-line metastatic urothelial carcinoma patients.
items.reason: EV+P treatment resulted in a significantly improved median overall survival compared to platinum-based chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the FDA approval of EV+P.
items.reference: Cancer of the Bladder and Urinary Tract · TREATMENT APPROACHES (part 3)
